definition: Percentage of samples that ignited during the experimental burning. A sample is considered ignited when a flame appears after being exposed to an ignition source during a limited period of time and sustains the flame after the ignition source has been removed.
Index defined as a function of the ignition frequency and the mean ignition time score. Low = scores 0-1, medium = scores 2-3, high = scores 4-5.
Temperature of the sample (or surrounding air) at the beginning of the flame phase (when flames appear and are maintained).
Temperature of the sample (or surrounding air) at the beginning of the smoke phase (when smoke appears).
Temperature of the sample (or surrounding air) at the beginning of the smouldering phase (when glowing occurs).
Time to the beginning of the flaming phase. Time starts when the sample is exposed to an ignition source or reaches a given temperature.

var_name: time to maximum heat release rate (s)
definition: Time elapsed since the beginning of the flaming phase up until the maximum heat release rate is reached.
Time elapsed since exposure to the ignition source up until the maximum smoke density is reached.
Time to the beginning of the smoke phase. Time starts when the sample is exposed to an ignition source or reaches a given temperature.
Time to the beginning of the smouldering phase (when glowing occurs). Time starts when exposed to ignition source or reaches a given temperature.
The amount of energy released per unit of fuel biomass burnt.
The rate of energy release during combustion per surface area unit.
The rate of energy release during combustion. Usually the average heat release rate over the experimental burning.
Maximum flame height, estimated visually to the nearest centimeter.
Maximum heat release rate per meter of fire front.
Number of opposite directions in which flames spread from the center of the sample (0 to 4).
Energy released as heat during flame occurrence, estimated as the area under the temperature-time curve throughout flaming duration divided by fresh fuel biomass.
Burnt biomass divided by the flaming duration (from ignition to flame extinction).

var_name: mass loss rate per area (g/m2 s)
definition: Mass loss rate per area unit of the fuel sample.
Maximum rate of energy release during combustion per surface area unit.
Maximum energy release rate obtained during the experimental burning.
Highest temperature measured in the flame during sample burning.
Highest temperature measured in the sample during burning.
Burnt biomass percentage divided by flaming duration (from ignition to flame extinction).
Volumetric smoke flow rate through the duct of a cone calorimeter.
Instantaneous amount of smoke produced per mass unit of burnt sample in a cone calorimeter.
Maximum rate of temperature increase during flaming combustion.
Amount of time that the combustion is sustained; can be restricted to flaming duration or include the smouldering phase.
Time elapsed from the appearance of the first visible flame until no more flames were seen.
Flaming duration standardized by the dry, pre-burning fuel mass.
Percentage of samples that maintained flames for at least a given time or propagated fire over at least a given distance.
Speed of burning (smouldering or flaming). Calculated by dividing the length of sample burnt by the burning time.
Amount of time over which smoke is emitted.
Amount of time during which glowing occurs, from end of last visible flame until glowing dies out.
Smouldering duration standardized by the dry, pre-burning fuel mass.
Post-burning sample weight related to its weight before the experimental burning. Initial weight change corresponds to evaporation of water and other gases.
Visually estimated percentage of the fuel biomass or volume consumed by the fire.
Total heat produced by the burning fuel over the entire experiment period, calculated by integrating the heat release rate curve vs. time.
Smoke production in a cone calorimeter standardized by the burnt specimen's area unit.
Compound value of flammability obtained by adding standardized scores of max sample temperature, rate of burning spread, and burnt biomass. Range: 0 (no flammability) to ~3 (maximum flammability).
